title: For discretionary-systematic hybrid traders: how do you encode "confirmation" rules that are obvious to your eye but hard to formalize?
author: kwame1776
selftext:
I run a rules-based discretionary strategy and I've been building a system to enforce it — the kind of thing where you describe your plan and an AI agent checks setups against it before you act.

The part I keep getting stuck on: encoding rules that are visually obvious but resist clean formalization. The rules either over-fit to recent trades or end up being so broad it passes everything.

For those who've gone down this road, do you encode soft confirmation rules at all, or keep them as a manual pre-flight checklist the human still runs? And if you do encode them, how do you make them strict enough to be useful without killing the discretionary judgment that made the rule worth having?

comments.author: StorageWeekly6982
comments.body: Usually the sweet spot is encoding the objective structure/risk rules, then keeping the softer “does this feel clean?” confirmation as a manual filter, IMO..

replies.author: kwame1776
replies.body: That maps closely to what I've landed on too. Structure, risk params, invalidation levels, encodes cleanly. The "does this feel clean" part I've stopped trying to automate and instead treat as a forcing function: the system flags what it can check, and if I'm overriding the soft stuff I have to say why. The audit trail ends up being as useful as the check itself.

comments.author: RemoraEdge
comments.body: Make it less discretionary so the algo can formalize it

comments.author: Fun-Society-1763
comments.body: This is the holy grail of hybrid trading! Getting an LLM to act as a strict risk-manager/setup-checker based on natural language rules is super powerful. The trick is framing your prompt to force binary (yes/no) answers based on specific chart context rather than open-ended analysis. I'd highly recommend checking out QuantPlace if you're trying to build this out. It's built specifically for these kinds of agentic workflows where you can have an AI validate your discretionary setups against a hard-coded set of rules before you pull the trigger.
